ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for pastry-wrapped glutinous rice cake; closest match is glutinous rice cake food product (FOODON_03302050). Pastry wrapping and frying are not captured in this code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Rice cake) is the closest defensible anchor. The crispy pastry coating and deep-frying preparation are not represented in this code. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for this composite preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for glutinous rice cake in crispy pastry. SNOMED CT does not have a specific concept for this composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one piece of glutinous rice cake in crispy pastry, weighing approximately 80 grams, commonly consumed as a snack or dessert during Chinese New Year or at dim sum.

nutrient_source.title: Glutinous Rice Cake In Crispy Pastry — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 酥皮年糕 Glutinous Rice Cake In Crispy Pastry (cr_id: G00010042) with confidence 0.95. All per_100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of glutinous rice and glutinous rice-based products — analogous estimate based on published GI values for glutinous rice (GI ~98) from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for glutinous rice cake in crispy pastry as a composite dish. GI of 98 is based on the well-established GI of glutinous rice (sticky rice) as the dominant carbohydrate component, sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of Glycemic Index (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). The pastry coating adds refined carbohydrates and fat; fat content may modestly attenuate the GI in practice, but the dominant glutinous rice component drives a high GI classification. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (98 × 25.19 × 0.80) / 100 ≈ 19.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g with partial offset from potassium 327 mg/100g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 231 mg/100g is borderline for hypertension management; however, the potassium-to-sodium ratio is favourable at approximately 1.4:1, which provides partial DASH-diet offset — limit portion to one piece and avoid additional salty condiments to keep total sodium within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index (~98) from glutinous rice with 25.2 g carbohydrate per 100g causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Glutinous rice cake has one of the highest GI values of any staple food (~98); even a single 80g piece delivers approximately 20g of rapidly absorbed carbohydrate, making this food unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes and should be avoided or consumed only in very small portions with close blood glucose monitoring.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried pastry preparation likely contributes saturated fat and AGEs; cholesterol reported as 0 mg/100g but fat quality is a concern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The crispy pastry preparation typically uses lard or shortening, contributing saturated fat and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) from high-heat frying; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption and prefer baked or steamed nian gao preparations to reduce saturated fat and AGE exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates AGEs and likely contributes trans fat from shortening/lard; glutinous rice has a high glycaemic load promoting indirect inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The deep-fried pastry preparation generates pro-inflammatory AGEs and likely contributes saturated and trans fat from lard or shortening; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this preparation method and choose steamed nian gao instead.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Glutinous Rice Cake in Crispy Pastry (酥皮年糕, Sū Pí Nián Gāo) is a traditional Chinese-Singaporean festive snack most commonly associated with Chinese New Year celebrations. It consists of a slice of nian gao — a dense, chewy, sweetened glutinous rice cake made from glutinous rice flour and brown sugar — encased in a thin, flaky pastry shell and deep-fried or pan-fried until golden and crisp. The result is a compelling textural contrast: a shatteringly crisp, golden exterior that gives way to a soft, sticky, mildly sweet interior. The dish is served warm and is prized for its nostalgic flavour and festive symbolism, as nian gao (年糕) literally means 'year cake' and is associated with prosperity and advancement in the coming year. Nutritionally, per 100g the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5g protein, 4.21g fat, and 25.19g carbohydrate with 3.7g dietary fibre and 0.21g sugar. Sodium is 231 mg/100g and potassium is 327 mg/100g. The glycemic index is very high (~98) due to the glutinous rice base, making this food clinically significant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The pastry preparation using lard or shortening introduces saturated fat and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) from high-heat frying, which are relevant for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis management. Cholesterol is reported as 0 mg/100g per the HPB Singapore Foodo database. This dish is not suitable for diabetics and should be consumed in strict moderation by those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease.

GI is estimated from the well-established glutinous rice GI value (~98) from the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of Glycemic Index (Atkinson et al., 2008), which is the closest defensible approved-source analogue for the dominant carbohydrate component.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010042) as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat data is not available from the HPB source for this record. 2. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and water content data for this dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of 2026-04-16. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as reported by HPB Singapore Foodo; this is confirmed zero per the approved source. 4. GI of 98 is an analogous estimate based on glutinous rice (sticky rice) GI from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. No direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources. 5. is_halal set to false as the traditional preparation uses lard (pork fat); halal-certified versions may exist but cannot be confirmed from the HPB source record. 6. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for pastry-wrapped deep-fried glutinous rice cake.
